content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 20, 1992


Peter M. Douglas
Executive Director
California Coastal Commission
45 Fremont Street, Suite 2000
San Francisco, CA  94105-2219

                                    Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                         Our File No. I-92-044 and I-92-058

Dear Mr. Douglas:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning the application of the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹  Specifically, your
letter requests guidance concerning the application of Section
87103.6 and Regulation 18704.6 to a proposed administrative
structure the Coastal Commission is considering establishing in
connection with the independent monitoring and adaptive management
program of the marine resource mitigation projects of the San
Onofre Nuclear Generating Station ("SONGS"). Because your request
seeks general guidance about the Coastal Commission's course of
conduct, we are treating your request as one for informal as-
sistance.²

                              QUESTION

     Does the proposed administrative structure meet the intended
purpose of Regulation 18704.6, as well as Section 87103.6, by
creating an adequate buffer between the permit applicant, whose
mitigation fees would pay for the employment of consultants and
contractors, and the consultants and contractors, whose work would
consist of reviewing and monitoring the permit applicant's
mitigation projects?  More specifically, does the proposed

_______________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

Our File Nos. I-92-044 and I-92-053
Page 2


administrative structure for the monitoring, management, and
oversight program for the SONGS marine resource mitigation
projects preclude the permit applicant, Southern California
Edison, from being considered the "source of income" to the
consultants and contractors retained by the Coastal Commission to
review and monitor Southern California Edison's mitigation
projects?

CONCLUSION

The administrative structural arrangement you present does
not violate section 87103.6. It meets all the criteria
enunciated by the Commission in connection with its prior adoption
of Regulation 18704.6, thereby avoiding the "source of income"
problem.

FACTS

The California Coastal Commission authorized construction of
Southern California Edison's and San Diego Gas & Electric's
Units 2 and 3 of the San Onofre Nuclear Generating Station (SONGS)
in northern San Diego County. At the time of the approval, the
Coastal Commission lacked sufficient information on the nature and
extent of the impact of the power plant operations on the marine
environment. To address this lack of information, the permit, No.
183-73, established a Marine Review Committee (MRC) to study the
effects of Units 2 and 3 on the marine environment.

On July 16, 1991, in response to the final report submitted
by MRC, the Coastal Commission adopted a resolution to further
condition permit No. 183-73. Because the MRC found that the
operations of Units 2 and 3 impacted the marine environment, the
Coastal Commission required the establishment of three new
mitigation projects to address such impacts: wetlands restoration
to compensate for fish impacts, behavioral barriers to redirect fish
impingement losses, and artificial reef construction to reduce
lost resources at the San Onofre Kelp Bed. The Coastal Commission
further required the applicant of the permit, Southern California
Edison, to have the primary responsibility for designing,
implementing, and funding these mitigation projects.

Finally, because the Coastal Commission was uncertain as to
whether the mitigation projects would successfully meet
performance standards identified in the permit, the Coastal
Commission additionally required the establishment of an
independent, third-party monitoring, management, and remediation
program with Coastal Commission oversight. This adaptive
management program would evaluate the progress of the mitigation

Our File Nos. I-92-044 and I-92-058
Page 3


projects and determine remedial steps, if necessary.3 The Coastal
Commission required the applicant of the permit, Southern
California Edison, to provide all funding necessary for the
monitoring and management program and the Commission's oversight
responsibilities.

In order to carry out the monitoring, management, and
oversight program, the Coastal Commission intends to retain at
least two primary consultants to provide the scientific and
technical expertise needed to oversee the mitigation projects and
other consultants and contractors to implement the monitoring
studies. These consultants will be paid from funds provided by
the applicant of the permit, Southern California Edison.

Furthermore, the Coastal Commission envisions setting up the
following administrative structure. The Coastal Commission would
enter into a memorandum of agreement ("MOA") with an appropriate
foundation or nonprofit organization to administer the monitoring,
management, and oversight program for the Coastal Commission. This MOA would establish the terms under which the
foundation or nonprofit would provide services to the Commission.4
These services would include (1) contracting for consultants and
other contractors, subject to the Commission's direction and
approval; and (2) establishing a special deposit account to
receive funds from Southern California Edison to cover the costs
of the SONGS monitoring, management and oversight program. These
costs would include the compensation paid to the consultants
retained by the Coastal Commission to work on the program.


3     For example, the Commission would retain responsibility for
approving the restoration and construction plans, and for
determining whether the goals specified in the approved plans and
the performance standards contained in the permit were met. If,
the Commission determined the mitigation projects were not
accomplishing the goals and standards, it could require remedial
measures. The Commission would also convene interagency
Wetland Advisory Panel and a Scientific Advisory Panel to assist
in implementing the permit conditions.

4     For example, one entity which has expressed an interest in
performing these administrative functions for the Coastal
Commission is the San Diego State University Foundation (the
"Foundation"). The Foundation is a nonprofit corporation
designated as an auxiliary organization in the Education Code of
the State of California. The Foundation annually administers over
$50. It enables university faculty to engage in sponsored research
and education programs, offers a vehicle to accept, invest and
expand donated monies, and is the legal "umbrella" under which
many community-oriented activities operate.

Our File Nos. I-92-044 and I-92-053
Page 4


     In order to clarify the contracting provision above, on March
11, 1991, I spoke with Chris Parry, Deputy Manager, Energy & Ocean
resources Unit, California Coastal Commission. She informed
that all the services envisioned to be provided by the foundation
or nonprofit organization would be administrative only. For
example, though the foundation or nonprofit organization would be
assisting the Coastal Commission in locating qualified personnel,
the Coastal Commission would be making the final selection. The
consultants would contract directly with the Coastal Commission
and would work at the Coastal Commission's headquarters under the
supervision of the Coastal Commission's Manager and Deputy Manager
of the Energy and Ocean Resources Unit. Furthermore, though the
foundation or nonprofit organization would be handling the
payroll, funds would be drawn from the special account only upon
the authorization of the Executive Director of the Coastal
Commission. The amount of compensation would be determined by the
Coastal Commission.

     This MOA would be the basis of the Master Agreement entered
into by the Coastal Commission and the foundation or nonprofit
organization. One of the terms of the agreement would specify
that the compensation paid to the consultants and contractors from
this special fund would be dependent solely upon completion of the
work and not upon any agency action pertaining to the mitigation
projects.

     The Coastal Commission would enter into a separate MOA with
the applicant of the permit, Southern California Edison, to
establish the work program and budget for the SONGS monitoring,
management and oversight program. One of the terms of this
agreement would be to direct Southern California Edison5 to
deposit the necessary funds in a special Coastal Commission
account established for this purpose with the foundation or
nonprofit organization. Another term would specify the amounts
and schedule of payments to the special account. Other terms
would specify that the payment of any mitigation fees as required
by the permit conditions is not contingent on the hiring of any
specific person or consultant, is not dependent upon the outcome
of the consultants' work or their recommendations, and is not
dependent upon any further agency action.

                              ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official


_______________________________
     5 The term used throughout the letter was "permittee." According
to Dorothy Dickey, Counsel, California Coastal Commission, the
term is synonymous with the permit applicant, Southern California
Edison.

Our File Nos. I-92-044 and I-92-058
Page 5


position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A "public official" is defined
in Regulation 18700 to include every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. (Section 82048.) Based on the abbreviated job
descriptions of the consultant scientists, this definition would
include those persons who are retained under contract by the
Coastal Commission to review and monitor the permit applicant's
mitigation projects.

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on:

     (c) Any source of income aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

Therefore, as a general rule, any person or business that has
made any payment to a public official in the past 12 months is a
source of income to that official for the purposes of Section
87103(c). However, Section 82030(b)(2) provides an exception for
certain types of payments which are made to agency employees and
to certain consultants who are under contract to the public
agency.6 Specifically, salary and reimbursement for expenses or
per diem received from a public agency are exempted from the
definition of "income" and are, therefore, not considered "sources
of income" to agency employees and to certain consultants retained
by an agency.

The recently enacted Section 87103.6 provides another
limited exception:

     Notwithstanding subdivision (c) of Section
87103, any person who makes a payment to a state
agency or local government agency to defray the
estimated reasonable costs to process any
application, approval, or any other action, '
including but not limited to, holding public



_____________________________

6 Only consultants who are public officials as that term is
defined in the Act are subject to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act.

Our File Nos. I-92-044 and I-92-058
Page 6


hearings and evaluating or preparing any report or
document, shall not by reason of the payments be a
source of income to a person who is retained or
employed by the agency.

Therefore, generally speaking, any person who makes a payment
to a public agency to defray processing costs is exempted from the
definition of "source" and shall not by reason of the payments be
a "source of income" to an employee or a consultant who is
retained or employed by the agency.

Approximately 10 days prior to the enactment of Section
87103.6, the Commission had adopted Regulation 18704.6.
Regulation 18704.6 addressed the same problem as the statute and
also created a limited exemption for persons making payments to
public agencies for the purpose of defraying costs. The
regulation is narrow in scope and expressly establishes criteria
that must be met before the exemption may be applied.

The concern expressed in your letter is that the "permittee,"
Southern California Edison, should not be considered the "source
of income" under Section 87103.6 to the consultants and
contractors retained by the Coastal Commission, though the
permittee pays the fees for employing them. For that reason, your
inquiry addresses the appropriateness of the proposed
administrative structure envisioned by the Coastal Commission in
view of recently enacted Section 87103.6 and Regulation 18704.6.

The administrative structural arrangement you present does
not violate Section 87103.6. It meets all the criteria
enunciated by the Commission in connection with its prior adoption
of Regulation 18704.6, thereby avoiding the "source of income"
problem. (See, Meade Advice Letter, No. A-91-533.) As you
correctly concluded in your letter, under the master agreement
between the Coastal Commission and the foundation or nonprofit
organization, the Coastal Commission would retain authority to
determine which consultants and contractors are hired to work on
the monitoring and management activities for the mitigation
projects, to direct the work and evaluate the performance of those
consultants and contractors, and to establish the amount of
compensation paid. The consultants and contractors would be paid
from a special fund which is administered by the foundation or
nonprofit, and over which the applicant exercises no
control or authority. Funds could be drawn from the account only
upon the authorization of the Coastal Commission. Finally, terms
would be included in both MOAs to prohibit contingency
arrangements that could create a situation of bias for the
consultants and contractors retained by the Coastal Commission.

Our File Nos. I-92-044 and I-92-058
Page 7


     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Deanne Stone
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DRS:aa




_____________________________
7    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question:
Does the proposed administrative structure meet the intended purpose of Regulation 18704.6, as well as Section 87103.6, by creating an adequate buffer between the permit applicant, whose mitigation fees would pay for the employment of consultants and contractors, and the consultants and contractors, whose work would consist of reviewing and monitoring the permit applicant's mitigation projects? More specifically, does the proposed

---

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

administrative structure for the monitoring, management, and oversight program for the SONGS marine resource mitigation projects preclude the permit applicant, Southern California Edison, from being considered the "source of income" to the consultants and contractors retained by the Coastal Commission to review and monitor Southern California Edison's mitigation projects?
sections.conclusion: The administrative structural arrangement you present does not violate Section 87103.6. It meets all the criteria enunciated by the Commission in connection with its prior adoption of Regulation 18704.6, thereby avoiding the "source of income" problem.
sections.facts:
The California Coastal Commission authorized construction of Southern California Edison's and San Diego Gas and Electric's Units 2 and 3 of the San Onofre Nuclear Generating Station (SONGS) in northern San Diego County. At the time of the approval, the Coastal Commission lacked sufficient information on the nature and extent of the impact of the power plant operations on the marine environment. To address this lack of information, the permit, No. 183-73, established a Marine Review Committee ("MRC") to study the effects of Units 2 and 3 on the marine environment.

On July 16, 1991, in response to the final report submitted by MRC, the Coastal Commission adopted a resolution to further condition permit No. 183-73. Because the MRC found that the operations of Units 2 and 3 impacted the marine environment, the Coastal Commission required the establishment of three new mitigation projects to address such impacts: wetlands restoration to compensate for fish impacts, behavioral barriers to reduce fish impingement losses, and artificial reef construction to reduce lost resources at the San Onofre Kelp Bed. The Coastal Commission further required the applicant of the permit, Southern California Edison, to have the primary responsibility for designing, implementing, and funding these mitigation projects.

Finally, because the Coastal Commission was uncertain as to whether the mitigation projects would successfully meet performance standards identified in the permit, the Coastal Commission additionally required the establishment of an independent, third-party monitoring, management, and remediation program with Coastal Commission oversight. This adaptive management program would evaluate the progress of the mitigation

projects and determine remedial steps, if necessary.3 The Coastal Commission required the applicant of the permit, Southern California Edison, to provide all funding necessary for the monitoring and management program and the Commission's oversight responsibilities.

In order to carry out the monitoring, management, and oversight program, the Coastal Commission intends to retain at least two primary consultants to provide the scientific and technical expertise needed to oversee the mitigation projects and other consultants and contractors to implement the monitoring studies. These consultants will be paid from funds provided by the applicant of the permit, Southern California Edison.

Furthermore, the Coastal Commission envisions setting up the following administrative structure. The Coastal Commission would enter into a memorandum of agreement ("MOA") with an appropriate foundation or nonprofit organization to administer SONGS monitoring, management, and oversight program for the Coastal Commission. This MOA would establish the terms under which the foundation or nonprofit would provide services to the Commission.4 These services would include (1) contracting for consultants and other contractors, subject to the Commission's direction and approval; and (2) establishing a special deposit account to receive funds from Southern California Edison to cover the costs of the SONGS monitoring, management and oversight program. These costs would include the compensation paid to the consultants retained by the Coastal Commission to work on the program.

3 For example, the Commission would retain responsibility for approving the restoration and construction plans, and for determining whether the goals specified in the approved plans and the performance standards contained in the permit were met. If, the Commission determined the mitigation projects were not accomplishing the goals and standards, it could require remedial measures. The Commission would also convene an interagency Wetland Advisory Panel and a Scientific Advisory Panel to assist in implementing the permit conditions.

4 For example, one entity which has expressed an interest in performing these administrative functions for the Coastal Commission is the San Diego State University Foundation (the "Foundation"). The Foundation is a nonprofit corporation designated as an auxiliary organization in the Education Code of the State of California. The Foundation annually administers over $50. It enables university faculty to engage in sponsored research and education programs, offers a vehicle to accept, invest and expand donated monies, and is the legal "umbrella" under which many community-oriented activities operate.

In order to clarify the contracting provision above, on March 11, 1991, I spoke with Chris Parry, Deputy Manager, Energy & Ocean Resources Unit, California Coastal Commission. She informed me that all the services envisioned to be provided by the foundation or nonprofit organization would be administrative only. For example, though the foundation or nonprofit organization would be assisting the Coastal Commission in locating qualified personnel, the Coastal Commission would be making the final selection. The consultants would contract directly with the Coastal Commission and would work at the Coastal Commission’s headquarters under the supervision of the Coastal Commission’s Manager and Deputy Manager of the Energy and Ocean Resources Unit. Furthermore, though the foundation or nonprofit organization would be handling the payroll, funds would be drawn from the special account only upon the authorization of the Executive Director of the Coastal Commission. The amount of compensation would be determined by the Coastal Commission.

This MOA would be the basis of the Master Agreement entered into by the Coastal Commission and the foundation or nonprofit organization. One of the terms of the agreement would specify that the compensation paid to the consultants and contractors from this special fund would be dependent solely upon completion of the work and not upon any agency action pertaining to the mitigation projects.

The Coastal Commission would enter into a separate MOA with the applicant of the permit, Southern California Edison, to establish the work program and budget for the SONGS monitoring, management and oversight program. One of the terms of this agreement would be to direct Southern California Edison5 to deposit the necessary funds in a special Coastal Commission account established for this purpose with the foundation or nonprofit organization. Another term would specify the amounts and schedule of payments to the special account. Other terms would specify that the payment of any mitigation fees as required by the permit conditions is not contingent on the hiring of any specific person or consultant, is not dependent upon the outcome of the consultants’ work or their recommendations, and is not dependent upon any further agency action.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official

5 The term used throughout the letter was "permittee." According to Dorothy Dickey, Counsel, California Coastal Commission, the term is synonymous with the permit applicant, Southern California Edison.

position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A "public official" is defined in Regulation 18700 to include every natural person who is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency. (Section 82048.) Based on the abbreviated job descriptions of the consultant scientists, this definition would include those persons who are retained under contract by the Coastal Commission to review and monitor the permit applicant's mitigation projects.

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(c) Any source of income aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

Therefore, as a general rule, any person or business that has made any payment to a public official in the past 12 months is a source of income to that official for the purposes of Section 87103(c). However, Section 82030(b)(2) provides an exception for certain types of payments which are made to agency employees and to certain consultants who are under contract to the public agency.6 Specifically, salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a public agency are exempted from the definition of "income" and are, therefore, not considered "sources of income" to agency employees and to certain consultants retained by an agency.

The recently enacted Section 87103.6 provides another limited exception:

Notwithstanding subdivision (c) of Section 87103, any person who makes a payment to a state agency or local government agency to defray the estimated reasonable costs to process any application, approval, or any other action, including but not limited to, holding public

6 Only consultants who are public officials as that term is defined in the Act are subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.

hearings and evaluating or preparing any report or document, shall not by reason of the payments be a source of income to a person who is retained or employed by the agency.

Therefore, generally speaking, any person who makes a payment to a public agency to defray processing costs is exempted from the definition of "source" and shall not by reason of the payments be a "source of income" to an employee or a consultant who is retained or employed by the agency.

Approximately 10 days prior to the enactment of Section 87103.6, the Commission had adopted Regulation 18704.6. Regulation 18704.6 addressed the same problem as the statute and also created a limited exemption for persons making payments to public agencies for the purpose of defraying costs. The regulation is narrow in scope and expressly establishes criteria that must be met before the exemption may be applied.

The concern expressed in your letter is that the "permittee," Southern California Edison, should not be considered the "source of income" under Section 87103.6 to the consultants and contractors retained by the Coastal Commission, though the permittee pays the fees for employing them. For that reason, your inquiry addresses the appropriateness of the proposed administrative structure envisioned by the Coastal Commission in view of recently enacted Section 87103.6 and Regulation 18704.6.

The administrative structural arrangement you present does not violate Section 87103.6. It meets all the criteria enunciated by the Commission in connection with its prior adoption of Regulation 18704.6, thereby avoiding the "source of income" problem. (See, Meade Advice Letter, No. A-91-533.) As you correctly concluded in your letter, under the master agreement between the Coastal Commission and the foundation or nonprofit organization, the Coastal Commission would retain authority to determine which consultants and contractors are hired to work on the monitoring and management activities for the mitigation projects, to direct the work and evaluate the performance of those consultants and contractors, and to establish the amount of compensation paid. The consultants and contractors would be paid from a special fund which is administered by the foundation or nonprofit, and over which the permit applicant exercises no control or authority. Funds could be drawn from the account only upon the authorization of the Coastal Commission. Finally, terms would be included in both MOAs to prohibit contingency arrangements that could create a situation of bias for the consultants and contractors retained by the Coastal Commission.

---

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

administrative structure for the monitoring, management, and oversight program for the SONGS marine resource mitigation projects preclude the permit applicant, Southern California Edison, from being considered the "source of income" to the consultants and contractors retained by the Coastal Commission to review and monitor Southern California Edison's mitigation projects?

CONCLUSION: The administrative structural arrangement you present does not violate Section 87103.6. It meets all the criteria enunciated by the Commission in connection with its prior adoption of Regulation 18704.6, thereby avoiding the "source of income" problem.
